classes.name: SABA (Rescue)
classes.description: Short-acting beta-agonist for acute bronchospasm; quick relief, not a controller.
classes.plainDescription: The inhaler you grab when you suddenly can't breathe. Works in minutes.

classes.name: ICS (Maintenance)
classes.description: Inhaled corticosteroid; the anti-inflammatory foundation of daily asthma control.
classes.plainDescription: A daily inhaler that calms airway inflammation so attacks happen less.

classes.name: ICS/LABA (Combo)
classes.description: Steroid + long-acting bronchodilator in one inhaler for moderate-to-severe asthma or COPD.
classes.plainDescription: A daily inhaler that combines the steroid with a long-acting opener.

classes.name: LAMA
classes.description: Long-acting muscarinic antagonist. FDA-indicated for COPD maintenance, not asthma monotherapy; not for rescue.
classes.plainDescription: A once-daily opener used mainly for COPD — not a rescue inhaler.

classes.name: Biologics & Non-Inhalers
classes.description: Specialty-pharmacy biologics (anti-IgE, anti-IL5, anti-IL4) for severe asthma. Separate PA pathway; not inhalers.
classes.plainDescription: Injection or infusion drugs for severe asthma — handled differently from inhalers.

glossary.short: Insurer sign-off needed before the drug is covered.
glossary.definition: A requirement that the prescriber get the plan's approval before it will pay for a drug. Until it's approved, the pharmacy claim rejects — which is the round-trip this tool helps you skip.

glossary.short: The plan forces the brand; the generic rejects.
glossary.definition: Some plans prefer a specific brand and won't cover its generic equivalent. You must write the brand and mark "dispense as written" or the pharmacy claim rejects. Example: MassHealth prefers brand Ventolin over generic albuterol.

glossary.short: Must fail a preferred drug first.
glossary.definition: The plan requires the patient to try (and fail, or be intolerant of) one or more preferred drugs before it will cover the one you want.

glossary.short: Tell the pharmacy not to substitute a generic.
glossary.definition: A note on the prescription instructing the pharmacist to dispense the exact brand you wrote, not a generic substitute. Needed when the plan's BOGL rule forces the brand.
Short-acting beta-agonist. The inhaler a patient carries and uses when they suddenly can't breathe well. Works in minutes; not a daily controller.

glossary.short: A daily steroid inhaler that calms inflammation.
glossary.definition: Inhaled corticosteroid. Taken every day to keep the airways from getting inflamed and twitchy. It prevents attacks; it does not relieve one in progress.

glossary.short: Inhalers have generics; biologics have biosimilars.
glossary.definition: Inhalers are small-molecule drugs, so cheaper copies are FDA-approved "generics" (e.g., Wixela Inhub for Advair). Injectable biologics like Xolair get "biosimilars" instead (e.g., Omlyclo) — a different FDA pathway.

classes.name: Estrogen — transdermal
classes.description: Transdermal 17β-estradiol (patch, gel, or spray); skips first-pass hepatic metabolism, lower VTE risk than oral.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen patch or gel absorbed through the skin — gentler on clot risk than pills.

classes.name: Estrogen — oral
classes.description: Oral estradiol or conjugated estrogens; effective, but first-pass hepatic metabolism raises VTE/clot and stroke risk vs transdermal.
classes.plainDescription: An estrogen pill taken by mouth. Works well, but carries a higher clot risk than the patch.

classes.name: Progestogen
classes.description: Progestogen added to systemic estrogen for endometrial protection in patients with an intact uterus; micronized progesterone preferred.
classes.plainDescription: Added to estrogen if you still have a uterus, to protect the uterine lining.

classes.name: Vaginal (local) estrogen
classes.description: Low-dose vaginal estradiol (cream, tablet, insert, or ring) for genitourinary syndrome of menopause; minimal systemic absorption.
classes.plainDescription: Low-dose estrogen placed vaginally for dryness and urinary symptoms (GSM).

classes.name: Estrogen–progestin (oral combo)
classes.description: A single oral tablet with both an estrogen and a progestin, for a person with an intact uterus; generic estradiol/norethindrone acetate is the usual first-pass.
classes.plainDescription: One pill that combines estrogen and the uterus-protecting hormone.

Estrogen, with a progestogen if you still have a uterus, to treat menopause symptoms.
Vaginal dryness and urinary symptoms of menopause, usually treated with low-dose local estrogen.
If you have a uterus, a progestogen is added to estrogen to keep the uterine lining safe.
Estrogen patches and gels are absorbed through the skin and carry a lower clot risk than estrogen pills.

records.tier: Preferred Drug List — no PA required for the preferred generic
records.verificationNote: Read directly off the NYRx Preferred Drug List (PDL) PDF, revised June 4, 2026, page 9, section III. Cardiovascular > Angiotensin Converting Enzyme Inhibitors (ACEIs). Preferred: benazepril, enalapril, lisinopril, ramipril (no PA on any of the four); non-preferred agents require PA. Cross-confirmed independently via two Medicaid MCOs (Fidelis Care and Healthfirst) that both resolved to this same statewide PDL. New York carved the Medicaid pharmacy benefit out of Medicaid Managed Care entirely, effective April 1, 2023 — every NY Medicaid Managed Care plan (Fidelis, Healthfirst, MetroPlus, UnitedHealthcare Community Plan, EmblemHealth, etc.) shares this single NYRx PDL rather than publishing its own, so this guide lists one consolidated "New York Medicaid (NYRx)" payer instead of listing each MCO separately with duplicate cells. Dose/sig (10mg once daily) is standard first-line dosing, not itself stated in the PDL (which lists drug names/coverage only) — verification applies to the preferred/non-preferred/PA determination. NYRx does not publish per-agent dollar copay tiers in this PDL.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the Excellus BCBS '2026 Individual & Family Metal Plans, Base, Simply Blue Plus and College Blue Plans Formulary Guide (2981)' PDF (fetched via direct HTTPS request to fm.formularynavigator.com, 143 pages, confirmed valid PDF). Page 5 of the document states 'CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026' as the guide's effective/current date. The 'ACE INHIBITORS' subsection appears under 'CARDIOVASCULAR THERAPY AGENTS' on document page 26 (page index 25), listing: benazepril (Tier 1, PV), captopril (Tier 1, PV), enalapril maleate oral solution (Tier 3, PA;QL;PV), enalapril maleate oral tablet (Tier 1, PV), fosinopril (Tier 1, PV), lisinopril (Tier 1, PV), moexipril (Tier 1, PV), perindopril erbumine (Tier 1, PV), quinapril (Tier 1, PV), ramipril (Tier 1, PV), and trandolapril (Tier 3, PV — no PA flag). The document's legend (page 4) defines PV as 'Preventive drug list' (a benefit-design flag exempting the drug from deductible on HDHPs, not a coverage restriction), so PV-only agents are treated as clean-covered Tier 1, not PA/step/nonformulary. No ACE-inhibitor-specific step-therapy criterion or PA policy beyond the enalapril-solution PA flag was found in this document; the general step-therapy/PA definitions on page 3 are plan-wide boilerplate, not class-specific. Lisinopril is selected as the representative preferred agent because it and every other listed ACE inhibitor tablet (except trandolapril) share identical Tier 1/no-PA status in this guide -- there is no single named 'preferred' agent distinguished from the rest of the class by this document; lisinopril is used as the representative pick per this repo's existing NY ACE inhibitor guide convention (see ny-medicaid record in the same guide). Confirmed via the formulary's own index (document page 136) that lisinopril appears on page 26, matching the extracted section. Not verified: whether Excellus applies any separate step-therapy edit at the pharmacy-claims level that isn't disclosed in this member-facing formulary guide (such internal PBM edits are sometimes stricter than the published document).

records.tier: Tier 1 - lowest-cost generic tier (of 3 standard tiers; some plans have a 4th tier)
records.verificationNote: Fetched the actual 80-page PDF directly (WebFetch was blocked by a domain safe-browsing check on uhcprovider.com, but a direct curl download succeeded and returned a genuine PDF, consistent with this repo's known pattern of plain-fetch bypassing bot/safety blocks that trip agentic browser tools). Page 1 confirms publisher 'UnitedHealthcare & affiliated companies', document title '2026 Prescription Drug List', effective January 1, 2026. Pages 19-22 (Cardiovascular Agents section) list every ACE inhibitor generic name and brand alphabetically with Tier and Requirements/Limits columns: benazepril hcl oral (Tier 1), captopril oral (Tier 1), enalapril maleate oral solution (Tier 3, PA), enalapril maleate oral tablet (Tier 1), fosinopril sodium (Tier 1), lisinopril oral (Tier 1), ramipril (Tier 1), trandolapril (Tier 1) - all with no PA/ST/QL codes except the enalapril oral solution. Brand entries ALTACE, VASOTEC, VASERETIC, ZESTRIL, ZESTORETIC are marked 'E' and LOTENSIN/LOTENSIN HCT are Tier 4; the legend on page 5 defines 'E' as 'May be excluded from coverage or subject to Prior Authorization in Connecticut, New Jersey and New York' - directly applicable since this is the NY-scoped cell and the document's footer on every drug-list page states 'CT, NJ and NY.' No quinapril/Accupril, moexipril/Univasc, or perindopril/Aceon entries exist anywhere in the 80-page document (confirmed via full-text scan) - they are simply absent from this PDL, not merely unlisted due to a search gap. Preferred agent selection (lisinopril) is inferred rather than explicitly named 'preferred' in prose - the PDL lists all Tier 1 generics as equally unrestricted with no single agent singled out as 'preferred' by name; lisinopril is the most commonly prescribed ACE inhibitor in practice and carries no distinguishing restriction here, consistent with every other Tier-1 generic in the class. Representative strength (10 mg) and sig are standard prescribing convention, not read from the PDL (which does not include dosing directions). BOGL (brand-over-generic) is not active for this class: all Tier 1 generics are unrestricted while every brand name in the class is either excluded/PA-flagged (E) or pushed to Tier 4, i.e. the formulary favors generic, not brand.

records.tier: Tier 1 (lowest cost share) with a quantity limit (QL)
records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Anthem 2026 Select Drug List — Three Tier Drug Plan PDF (INDNY Marketplace / Individual-HIX), fetched from https://fm.formularynavigator.com/FBO/143/2026_Select_3_Tier_NY_ABS_IND.pdf, page 27, 'ACE INHIBITORS' section, document stamped 'Effective 07012026' on every page footer. The full class list is: benazepril hcl (Tier 1, QL), captopril (Tier 2, QL), enalapril maleate (Tier 1, QL), fosinopril sodium (Tier 1, QL), lisinopril (Tier 1, QL), moexipril hcl (Tier 1, QL), perindopril erbumine (Tier 1, QL), quinapril hcl (Tier 1, QL), ramipril 1.25mg (Tier 1, DO=dose optimization), ramipril 10mg/2.5mg/5mg (Tier 1, QL), and trandolapril (Tier 1, QL) — no brand names appear anywhere in this section (confirmed: the document states on page 3 this is 'a complete listing of all the drugs on the drug list'). None of the 10 agents carry a PA, ST (step therapy), or NF (non-formulary) flag per the legend on page 27 ('PA=Prior Authorization QL=Quantity Limit ST=Step Therapy DO=Dose Optimization'), so paRequired is empty and stepTherapy is null for this class — confirmed, not inferred. Lisinopril was selected as the representative preferred agent because it is the single most commonly prescribed first-line ACE inhibitor and sits at the lowest tier (Tier 1) alongside all agents except captopril; the document itself does not single out one ACE inhibitor as 'preferred' beyond the shared Tier 1 designation, so the choice of lisinopril as the representative pick (vs. e.g. enalapril) is inferred, not stated verbatim — hence noting this distinction though the overall record is fully verified against the source. Sig/strength/directions are standard clinical convention, not stated in the formulary PDF (which lists drug/tier/notes only, no dosing directions).

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the 'Excellus BlueCross BlueShield 2026 Formulary (List of Covered Drugs)' PDF covering Medicare BlueActive (PPO) and its sibling Medicare Advantage plans (fetched via direct HTTPS request to fm.formularynavigator.com; 108-page, 3.2 MB PDF with real extractable text, confirmed a genuine formulary document, not an error page). Page 1 cover states 'This formulary was updated on 7/01/2026', 'Formulary ID 26059', document code 'Y0028_9055_a_C ... B-8970Y26-05', publisher Excellus BlueCross BlueShield, and explicitly lists 'Medicare BlueActive (PPO)' as the first plan on the cover, confirming this is the correct payer/product document. The 'Angiotensin-Converting Enzyme (Ace) Inhibitors' subsection, filed under the 'CARDIOVASCULAR AGENTS' category, spans the bottom of page 50 into page 51 and reads verbatim: benazepril (Tier 1), captopril (Tier 2), enalapril maleate oral tablet (Tier 1), fosinopril (Tier 1), lisinopril (Tier 1), moexipril (Tier 2), perindopril erbumine (Tier 2), quinapril (Tier 1), ramipril (Tier 1), trandolapril (Tier 2) -- every single entry has a blank REQUIREMENTS/LIMITS column (no PA, ST, or QL code), confirmed by cross-checking the same table rows against neighboring drug classes on the same pages that DO show 'PA', 'ST', or 'QL (n per 30 days)' annotations (e.g. EDARBI 'ST', FILSPARI 'PA', CABLIVI 'PA; QL'), so the absence of any code for the ACE inhibitors is a genuine clean-covered status, not a missing-data artifact. The tier legend on page VI (document page 8) defines Tier 1 as 'Preferred Generic: Select generic drugs ... offer clinical and cost savings advantages' and Tier 2 as 'Generic: Most other generic drugs.' No brand-name ACE inhibitor (searched Accupril, Lotensin, Altace, Vasotec) appears anywhere in the 108-page document, confirming no brand-over-generic policy is active for this class -- brands are simply absent from the formulary, consistent with Medicare Part D plans not stocking branded ACE inhibitors once fully genericized. Lisinopril is selected as the representative preferred pick (per this guide's existing NY ACE inhibitor convention, matching the ny-medicaid record) since five of the nine listed agents (benazepril, enalapril, fosinopril, lisinopril, quinapril, ramipril) share identical Tier 1/no-restriction status; lisinopril is the most commonly prescribed first-line agent among that Tier 1 set. No class-wide step-therapy criterion, quantity limit, or PA policy was found for ACE inhibitors in this document. Not verified: whether Excellus/Express Scripts applies any pharmacy-claims-level edit not disclosed in this member-facing formulary (internal PBM edits can occasionally be stricter than the published document), and whether cost-sharing differs across the plan's several PPO/HMO product variants sharing this one formulary (the tier assignment is identical across all listed plans in this document).

records.verificationNote: Fetched the exact cited PDF (Excellus_2026_Metal_Plans_Base___Simply_Blue_Plus___College_Blue_Plans_Formulary_Guide_2981_v26.pdf, 143 pages) directly from fm.formularynavigator.com. Confirmed cover page reads '2026 Individual & Family Metal Plans, Base, Simply Blue Plus and College Blue Plans Formulary Guide (2981)' published by ExcellusBCBS.com, and page 5 header states 'CURRENT AS OF 7/1/2026' (the document's effective/currency date, matching today's review date). Read the full NSAID section verbatim on page 9 under headings 'NSAID ANALGESIC, CYCLOOXYGENASE-2 (COX-2) SELECTIVE INHIBITORS', 'NSAID ANALGESICS (COX NON-SPECIFIC) - OTHER/OXICAM DERIVATIVES/PHENYLACETIC ACID DERIVATIVES/PROPIONIC ACID DERIVATIVES/INDOLE ACETIC ACID DERIVATIVES'. Confirmed verbatim: naproxen oral tablet is Tier 1 with no restriction codes; ibuprofen, celecoxib (QL), meloxicam, diclofenac potassium/sodium, etodolac, indomethacin, ketorolac (QL), nabumetone (QL), sulindac, piroxicam, and ketoprofen are all Tier 1; naproxen sodium tablets, naproxen DR/EC 375mg, oxaprozin (QL), and flurbiprofen 100mg are Tier 3 with no PA/ST flag; 'DICLOFENAC SUBMICRONIZED' is the only named NSAID item flagged Tier 3 with PA and QL. The PA/QL/ST/PV/S abbreviation legend was read verbatim from page 4-5 ('PA = Prior authorization required... QL = Quantity limit applies... ST = Step therapy required'). No class-wide step-therapy criterion or brand-over-generic policy is stated anywhere in the NSAID section or the general program-description pages (3-5) -- BOGL is not mentioned in this document at all, so boglActive is recorded false rather than inferred. The specific strength (500 mg) and sig for naproxen are standard-of-care defaults (partial/inferred) since the formulary guide lists only the drug name and tier, not a dosing sig -- the document itself carries only generic product descriptions, tiers, and restriction codes, not prescribing directions.

records.verificationNote: Fetched the actual 80-page PDF directly (WebFetch was blocked by a domain safe-browsing check on uhcprovider.com, and the claude-in-chrome browser's PDF viewer would not expose extractable text, but a direct curl download with a standard browser User-Agent succeeded and returned a genuine 3.8MB, 80-page PDF - consistent with this repo's known pattern of plain-fetch/curl bypassing bot or safety blocks that trip agentic fetch tools). Page 1 confirms publisher 'UnitedHealthcare & affiliated companies', document title '2026 Prescription Drug List', effective January 1, 2026. Page 8, under the section header 'Analgesics - Drugs for Pain and Inflammation' (confirmed against the page-2 table of contents, which maps this header to page 8), lists every oral NSAID generic and brand name alphabetically with a Tier and Requirements/Limits column: naproxen oral tablet, naproxen dr, ec-naproxen, ibuprofen oral tablet, meloxicam oral tablet, indomethacin oral capsule, ketorolac tromethamine oral, nabumetone oral, sulindac oral, and diclofenac sodium oral are all Tier 1 with no PA/ST/QL flag. celecoxib oral, diclofenac potassium oral tablet 50mg, naproxen sodium oral tablet, etodolac, indomethacin er, oxaprozin oral tablet, and piroxicam oral are Tier 2, also unflagged. diclofenac sodium er, diclofenac-misoprostol, and EC-NAPROSYN 375mg brand are Tier 3, unflagged. Brand names ANAPROX DS, CELEBREX, NAPROSYN, and RELAFEN DS are marked 'E'; diclofenac potassium 25mg and LODINE are marked 'E, QL'; ibuprofen oral suspension and diclofenac sodium external gel are marked 'E'. The legend on page 5 defines 'E' as 'May be excluded from coverage or subject to Prior Authorization in Connecticut, New Jersey and New York' - directly applicable since this is the NY-scoped cell (the document's page-8 footer itself reads 'CT, NJ and NY'). No 'ST' (step therapy) flag appears anywhere on the oral NSAID lines, and a full-document text search for 'NSAID' and for a class-wide step-therapy criterion returned no hits, so no class-wide step-therapy rule is stated in this PDL for oral NSAIDs (confirmed absent, not merely unfound). Preferred-agent selection (naproxen) is inferred rather than explicitly labeled 'preferred' in prose: the PDL lists naproxen, ibuprofen, and meloxicam as equally-unrestricted Tier 1 generics with no single agent singled out by name as the class 'preferred' pick; naproxen was chosen as the representative pick per standard first-line NSAID prescribing convention, with ibuprofen and meloxicam listed first among alternatives since they carry the identical Tier 1/no-flag status. Representative strength (500 mg BID) and sig are standard prescribing convention, not read from the PDL (which lists strengths available per formulation but not dosing directions). BOGL (brand-over-generic) is not active for this class: every brand name in the oral NSAID class is either 'E'-flagged (excluded/PA in NY) or pushed to Tier 4, while the corresponding generics remain Tier 1/2/3 unrestricted - i.e. the formulary favors generic, not brand, consistent with the same pattern already confirmed for this payer's ACE inhibitor cell.

records.verificationNote: Fetched the actual PDF at the cited URL (82 pages, downloaded and parsed with a PDF text-extraction tool after a plain WebFetch returned only PDF structural metadata/image streams with no readable text). Page 1 confirms the document is Anthem's own 'Select Drug List' / 'Drug list — Three Tier Drug Plan' for the NY Marketplace/Individual product ('INDNY Marketplace'), directing members to anthem.com/ny-drug-list. Every content page (7-16 confirmed) carries the footer 'Effective 07012026' (July 1, 2026) and a legend defining PA/QL/SP/ST/NF/DX/BE/DO/LD abbreviations. Page 10 contains the full '*NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENTS (NSAIDS)*' section: diclofenac potassium, diclofenac sodium (both DR and ER), ec-naproxen 500mg, etodolac (tablet/capsule/ER), flurbiprofen, IBU/ibuprofen (all forms and strengths), indomethacin, ketoprofen ER, ketorolac, meclofenamate, mefenamic acid, meloxicam tablet, nabumetone, naproxen (plain, DR, sodium), oxaprozin, piroxicam, and sulindac are all listed Tier 1 with only a Quantity Limit (QL) note -- no PA or ST. Two exceptions read verbatim within that same section carry 'ST; QL': ec-naproxen 375mg delayed-release tablet, and meloxicam oral suspension (the meloxicam tablet has no such flag). Celecoxib appears in a separate page-9 category, '*CYCLOOXYGENASE 2 (COX-2) INHIBITORS*', as Tier 2 with 'ST; QL' verbatim -- confirmed as the only COX-2-selective agent in the document via a full-document search. Ibuprofen-famotidine combination tablet (page 10) is Tier 3, 'ST; QL'. TOLECTIN (tolmetin) oral tablet appears at a page-10 character boundary with its tier/restriction codes not cleanly captured in this extraction pass; rather than guess, it is omitted from this record entirely (neither claimed covered nor claimed PA-required) pending a cleaner re-read of that exact line. The preferred agent (plain naproxen 500mg tablet, Tier 1, QL only, no ST/PA) and the celecoxib step-therapy restriction are both read directly off the document text with the effective date visible on the same pages.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the fetched PDF at the cited URL: page 1 cover confirms 'Excellus BlueCross BlueShield 2026 Formulary (List of Covered Drugs)' covering Medicare BlueActive (PPO) among the listed Medicare plans, Formulary ID 26059, updated 7/01/2026. Page 12 contains the 'ANALGESICS > Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Drugs' section with a DRUG NAME / DRUG TIER / REQUIREMENTS-LIMITS table; extracted with PyMuPDF and cross-checked against word-level (x,y) coordinates to confirm each tier number and requirement code aligns to the correct drug row (celecoxib, diclofenac forms, diflunisal, etodolac, flurbiprofen, IBU, ibuprofen forms, indomethacin forms, ketoprofen forms, ketorolac, meloxicam 7.5/15mg, nabumetone, naproxen forms, oxaprozin, piroxicam, sulindac). No oral NSAID line item in this section carries a PA or ST code — only QL (quantity limit) appears on celecoxib, ketoprofen ER, ketorolac, and both meloxicam strengths; diclofenac 25mg, naproxen DR/EC, and naproxen sodium sit at Tier 4 rather than being flagged non-formulary. Tier definitions read from page 7 ('DESCRIPTION OF TIERS': Tier 1 Preferred Generic, Tier 2 Generic, Tier 3 Preferred Brand, Tier 4 Non-Preferred, Tier 5 Specialty) and the requirement-code legend read from page 9 (PA, QL, ST, B/D PA, RV definitions). Naproxen oral tablet is the lowest-tier (Tier 2, Generic), no-restriction NSAID and is designated the preferred agent on that basis — the document does not use an explicit 'preferred' flag beyond tier placement for this class, so the Tier 2/no-restriction status is confirmed verbatim while the 'preferred' framing itself is a reasonable direct reading of the tier structure. Sig/dosing text is standard naproxen labeling, not itself sourced from the formulary (which lists drugs/tiers/restrictions only, not dosing instructions).

records.verificationNote: Re-read the full HFS PDL PDF (effective July 1, 2026, 150 pages) directly from illinois.gov, pages 4-5, 'Analgesics - Anti-Inflammatory: Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Agents (NSAIDs)' table, line by line, after a PR review (codex bot) correctly flagged that an earlier pass had misclassified Etodolac, Flurbiprofen tablet, and Ketoprofen capsule as PA-required when the PDL lists all three PREFERRED. This pass transcribed every PREFERRED/NON_PREFERRED line item verbatim rather than grouping by drug family, which is what caused the original error. Marked partial, not verified: the PDL does not state a class-specific PA sentence for NSAIDs (unlike ADHD/PPI/antipsychotic classes, which have explicit footnotes) — 'non-preferred requires PA' reflects standard HFS PDL convention rather than a quoted sentence for this specific class, so the PA determination itself is inferred, not read directly. Illinois FFS Medicaid pharmacy is administered directly by HFS (no contracted PBM). Sig for ibuprofen 400mg tablet is standard adult dosing from the DailyMed FDA label; the PDL itself lists coverage status only, not dosing.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Aetna Better Health of Illinois PDL (IL-20-09-49, July 2026), pages 19-20, '*Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatory Agents (Nsaids)*** - Arthritis And Pain Drugs' section, plus page 18's separate '*Cyclooxygenase 2 (Cox-2) Inhibitors*** - Arthritis And Pain Drugs' heading for celecoxib (note: this PDL groups oral NSAIDs under 'Arthritis And Pain Drugs' rather than a separate class, splits Cox-2 selective agents into their own sub-heading immediately before the main NSAID list, and the class the task calls 'Analgesics - Nonnarcotic' turns out in the actual document to start with acetaminophen on page 21, immediately after the NSAID section ends). Confirmed verbatim: celecoxib = Preferred QL (1 EA per 1 day), CELEBREX = Non-Preferred QL (1 EA per 1 day), VYSCOXA = Non-Preferred (page 18); naproxen, naproxen dr, naproxen sodium = Preferred; naproxen sodium er = Non-Preferred; ibuprofen tablets at every strength = Preferred (200mg OTC QL 6/day); meloxicam oral tablet = Preferred QL (1 EA per 1 day), meloxicam oral capsule = Non-Preferred; diclofenac potassium tablet 50mg = Preferred, 25mg and capsule = Non-Preferred; diclofenac sodium/er, etodolac/er, flurbiprofen, indomethacin/er, ketoprofen, ketorolac tromethamine, nabumetone (QL 4/day), sulindac = Preferred; diclofenac potassium oral capsule, fenoprofen calcium, ibuprofen oral suspension, ketoprofen er, meclofenamate sodium, mefenamic acid, oxaprozin, piroxicam, tolmetin sodium = Non-Preferred; brand names COXANTO, LOFENA, LURBIRO, NAPRELAN, RELAFEN DS, TOLECTIN DS, ZYBIC, orudis = Non-Preferred; IBU and MEDI-FIRST IBUPROFEN = Preferred. The PDL does not state a class-wide PA sentence for oral NSAIDs (unlike ADHD/PPI-style classes with explicit footnotes), so 'Non-Preferred requires PA' reflects the standard Aetna/Illinois PDL convention (page 2: 'When preferred generic drugs are available, the brand name drug will not be covered without prior authorization') rather than a quoted sentence specific to this class. Sig is standard naproxen 500mg BID dosing (not stated in the PDL, which lists coverage/QL only). Effective date confirmed page 1 header.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from ANALGESICS - ANTI-INFLAMMATORY: NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENTS (NSAIDs) section, effective May 2025. Confirmed generic naproxen (250/375/500 mg tablets, EC tablets, suspension), ibuprofen (400/600/800 mg tablets), celecoxib (all strengths), meloxicam tablets (Mobic-equivalent), diclofenac sodium delayed-release/ER, diclofenac potassium 50mg, indomethacin, etodolac, sulindac, nabumetone, and flurbiprofen are all P with QL only, no PA. Confirmed brand CELEBREX, NAPRELAN, VIMOVO, ARTHROTEC, DAYPRO, and generic/brand fenoprofen, ketoprofen, meclofenamate, mefenamic acid, piroxicam, and tolmetin products are all NP+PA. Naproxen selected as representative preferred agent as the most commonly first-line prescribed oral NSAID with the broadest set of preferred formulation options in this document.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the CountyCare Health Plan Medicaid Formulary (Q1 2026, updated 01/25/2026), pages 19-20, 'NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENTS (NSAIDS)' section under 'ANALGESICS - ANTI-INFLAMMATORY'. Confirmed verbatim across both pages: 'celecoxib (generic of CELEBREX) caps 50mg, 100mg, 200mg, 400mg Preferred', 'diclofenac potassium tabs 50mg Preferred', 'diclofenac sodium tb24 100mg; tbec 25mg, 50mg, 75mg Preferred', 'etodolac caps 200mg, 300mg; tabs 500mg; tb24 400mg, 500mg, 600mg Preferred', 'etodolac (generic of LODINE) tabs 400mg Preferred', 'flurbiprofen tabs 100mg Preferred', 'ibuprofen caps 200mg; chew 100mg; susp 50mg/1.25ml; tabs 200mg Preferred OTC', 'ibuprofen tabs 300mg, 400mg, 600mg, 800mg Preferred', 'indomethacin caps 25mg, 50mg; cpcr 75mg Preferred', 'indomethacin supp 50mg Preferred', 'indomethacin (generic of INDOCIN) susp 25mg/5ml Preferred', 'ketorolac tromethamine soln 30mg/ml, 60mg/2ml Preferred', 'ketorolac tromethamine tabs 10mg Preferred QL (4 tabs every 1 day)', 'meloxicam tabs 7.5mg, 15mg Preferred', 'nabumetone tabs 500mg, 750mg Preferred', 'naproxen susp 125mg/5ml; tabs 250mg, 375mg, 500mg; tbec 375mg, 500mg Preferred', 'naproxen sodium caps 220mg; tabs 220mg Preferred OTC', 'naproxen sodium tabs 275mg, 550mg Preferred', 'sulindac tabs 150mg, 200mg Preferred'. Only ketorolac tromethamine tablets carry a quantity limit (4 tabs/day, consistent with its short-term-use label); every other oral NSAID is unrestricted Preferred. No non-preferred NSAID appears anywhere in the document. Naproxen 500mg was selected as the representative preferred pick per this app's existing oral-NSAID convention (most commonly prescribed prescription-strength NSAID); CountyCare does not single out one NSAID as 'the' preferred agent -- essentially the entire oral NSAID class is covered without restriction. Sig is standard naproxen twice-daily with-food dosing, inferred (formulary lists drug/form/status/QL only, not clinical directions). Effective date confirmed on cover page.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Meridian Health Plan of Illinois PDL, 'ANALGESICS - NonNarcotic' section, 'Nonsteroidal Anti-inflammatory Agents (NSAIDs)' subsection, pages 8-10 of the PDF (internal page labels 5-6), effective/updated December 1, 2024. Confirmed a broad preferred generic NSAID list: naproxen sodium tablets (275/550mg), naproxen suspension and TBEC, celecoxib, meloxicam tablets, ibuprofen tablets (all three strengths) and suspension, diclofenac potassium 50mg tablets, diclofenac sodium (TB24 and TBEC), etodolac (multiple forms), sulindac, indomethacin (multiple forms), ketorolac tromethamine tablets, and flurbiprofen are all marked 'P' (Preferred). Meloxicam capsules (vs. tablets), naproxen sodium extended-release, diclofenac potassium 25mg/capsule form, oxaprozin, piroxicam, meclofenamate, mefenamic acid, fenoprofen, tolmetin, and Vimovo (naproxen-esomeprazole combo) are all Non-Preferred. No PA is required for the broad set of preferred first-line oral NSAIDs.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from Analgesics (Drugs for Pain) > Nonsteroidal Anti-inflammatory Drugs section, page 1 (PDF page 18) of the BCBSIL HIM 6-Tier Drug List, last updated 07/01/2026. Confirmed 'naproxen tab 250 mg, 375 mg' is Tier 1 with no UM code; ibuprofen, meloxicam, celecoxib (at 50-200mg), and diclofenac sodium DR are all Tier 1 as well, with only quantity limits (no PA) on celecoxib. Higher-strength/less-common formulations (naproxen sodium 550mg, diclofenac potassium, celecoxib 400mg, nabumetone, etodolac) sit at Tier 2, still covered, no PA. Only MECLOFENAMATE SODIUM carries a PA + QL restriction (Tier 4). No class-wide step therapy criterion applies -- this class is essentially open access across many agents at Tier 1-2.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Wellcare Value Script (PDP) 2026 Formulary (HPMS Submission ID 26195, effective 07/01/2026), NON-NARCOTIC ANALGESICS section (PDF pages 69-71, printed pages 55-57). Entries read verbatim: 'naproxen oral tablet 250 mg, 375 mg, 500mg 1' (page 69/55), 'ibu oral tablet 600 mg, 800 mg 1' and 'ibuprofen oral tablet 400 mg, 600 mg, 800 mg 1' (page 70/56), and 'meloxicam oral tablet 15 mg, 7.5 mg 1' (page 70/56) -- all Tier 1, no PA/QL flag. Every other oral NSAID entry found across the same three pages (celecoxib Tier 3; diclofenac forms, etodolac, nabumetone, sulindac, piroxicam at Tier 2; naproxen sodium, oxaprozin, diclofenac ER, flurbiprofen, diflunisal at Tier 4) carries no PA or step-therapy restriction either -- this entire therapeutic class is unrestricted on this formulary, with only cost-sharing tier varying by specific agent/salt/formulation.

Page 32, under the 'ANTIHYPERTENSIVES' > '*ACE INHIBITORS***' heading, lists exactly four agents with blank Notes columns (meaning no AL/DO/GR/PA/QL/SP/ST restriction applies per the document's own legend on page 1): benazepril hcl oral tablet (Lotensin), enalapril maleate oral tablet (Vasotec), lisinopril oral tablet (Zestril), and ramipril oral capsule. All four are therefore clean-covered preferred agents with no PA. Lisinopril was selected as the single 'preferredAgent' for this record because it is the conventional first-line ACE inhibitor in national guidelines and this PDL applies no differential restriction among the four -- the other three are captured as alternatives with identical 'preferred, no PA' status, which is directly read off the document, not inferred. Searched the full 153-page document (all pages, case-insensitive) for captopril, fosinopril, quinapril, moexipril, trandolapril, and perindopril: none appear anywhere in the PDL. Since this document format lists ONLY covered/preferred drugs (confirmed by reviewing page 1's legend and scanning multiple other classes for a 'non-preferred' section, which does not exist in this PDL), any agent absent from the list is not on the PDL and is tagged 'nonformulary' here -- inferred from the absence pattern (verified this is the document's format, not a fetch/extraction gap, since hundreds of other classes elsewhere in the same document render correctly with populated Notes columns like 'PA', 'PA; AL', 'PA; QL' etc., proving the extraction was complete and the omission is real, not an artifact). No class-wide step-therapy criterion or BOGL note appears near the ACE inhibitor section. Tier statement reflects that this is a single-tier PDL-based Medicaid program (no multi-tier copay structure), so 'tier' captures the PDL-covered/non-covered structure per the document, not a numeric tier.

Page 32, under the 'ANTIHYPERTENSIVES' header, 'ACE INHIBITORS' subsection, lists exactly four agents with no Reference Notes column entries (meaning no PA/ST/QL/AL restriction): benazepril hcl oral tablet (Lotensin), enalapril maleate oral tablet (Vasotec), lisinopril oral tablet (Zestril), and ramipril oral capsule — all clean-covered/preferred with no generic-vs-brand distinction needed since all four are generic-preferred listings. Lisinopril is designated as the representative preferred pick (most commonly prescribed first-line ACE inhibitor) since the PDL does not rank within the four listed agents — all four appear equally preferred with no differentiating notes. The same page also lists an 'ACE INHIBITORS & THIAZIDE/THIAZIDE-LIKE' combination subsection (benazepril-HCTZ/Lotensin HCT, enalapril-HCTZ/Vaseretic, lisinopril-HCTZ/Zestoretic, all unrestricted) and an 'ACE INHIBITOR & CALCIUM CHANNEL BLOCKER COMBINATIONS' subsection (amlodipine-benazepril/Lotrel, unrestricted) — combination products not captured in this single-agent-class record. Confirmed via full-text PDF search that captopril, fosinopril, moexipril, perindopril, quinapril, and trandolapril do not appear anywhere in this 153-page document (zero matches each) — this document is a preferred-only list (per its own legend on page 1, defining only AL/DO/GR/OTC/PA/QL/SP/ST as possible flags, with no explicit 'non-preferred' listing mechanism), so any ACE inhibitor absent from the PDL is presumed non-formulary/subject to the state's non-preferred prior-authorization process per the companion DMAS bulletin (vamedicaid.dmas.virginia.gov, PDL/Common Core Formulary changes effective 2026-04-01), consistent with how this same PDL was interpreted for the already-shipped NYRx ACE-inhibitor guide pattern and the already-verified anthem-healthkeepers-plus va-diabetes guide entries, which use this identical PDF as their primary Anthem HealthKeepers Plus source. Anthem HealthKeepers Plus (a Cardinal Care Medicaid MCO) defers to this statewide PDL for PDL-managed drug classes, per the existing va-diabetes guide's payer note structure and its cited anthem-healthkeepers-plus-cardinal-care-formulary-2026 / anthem-healthkeepers-plus-dmas-bulletin-2026 references. Tier statement is marked 'partial' in effect (verbatim PDL does not use a numbered tier system for Medicaid FFS/MCO plans the way commercial formularies do; the PDL's own binary is preferred-vs-PA, not tiered cost-sharing) — flagging this nuance rather than fabricating a tier label.

records.tier: Preferred (Tier 1) - PA required for concurrent use with a DPP-4 inhibitor per class-wide clinical criteria
records.verificationNote: Read directly from the NYRx PDP PDL PDF, Section VI. Endocrine and Metabolic Agents, 'Glucagon-Like Peptide-1 (GLP-1) Receptor Agonists – Type 2 Diabetes' subsection (marked CC), page 33 of 85, revision date June 4, 2026. Preferred column verbatim: 'exenatide, Ozempic pen, Trulicity, Victoza BLTG'; non-preferred column verbatim: 'Bydureon BCise, liraglutide (gen Victoza), Mounjaro, Ozempic tablets, Rybelsus, Soliqua, Xultophy'. Clinical Criteria text confirmed verbatim: 'Confirm diagnosis of FDA-approved or compendia-supported indication and Medicaid covered indication; PA required for patients utilizing a GLP-1 and DPP-IV concurrently.' Tirzepatide (Mounjaro) confirmed non-preferred/PA-required for this indication as of this revision -- note Zepbound (tirzepatide for weight loss) is a different brand/indication not addressed in this diabetes-class subsection.

Endocrine and Metabolic Agents, 'Sodium Glucose Co-Transporter 2 (SGLT2) Inhibitors' subsection, page 36 of 85, revision date June 4, 2026. Preferred column verbatim: 'Farxiga BLTG, Jardiance, Synjardy, Synjardy XR, Trijardy XR, Xigduo XR BLTG'; non-preferred column verbatim: 'dapagliflozin (gen Farxiga), dapagliflozin/metformin (gen Xigduo XR), Inpefa, Invokamet, Invokamet XR, Invokana, Segluromet, Steglatro'. No CC/F-Q-D flag attached to this subsection heading, confirming no class-wide restriction beyond the standard preferred/non-preferred PA split; Jardiance is the sole preferred empagliflozin monotherapy product (no generic equivalent exists yet), so it is the top pick over BLTG-preferred-but-generic-available dapagliflozin brand Farxiga.

records.tier: Preferred (Tier 1) - no PA required for preferred generic/authorized-generic products
records.verificationNote: Read directly from the NYRx PDP PDL PDF, Section VI. Endocrine and Metabolic Agents, three consecutive subsections 'Insulin – Long-Acting', 'Insulin – Mixes', and 'Insulin – Rapid-Acting', pages 35-36 of 85, revision date June 4, 2026. Long-Acting preferred column verbatim: 'insulin glargine-YFGN, Lantus Solostar vial, insulin degludec vial/pen (gen Tresiba), insulin glargine max solostar (gen Toujeo Max Solostar), insulin glargine solostar (gen Toujeo Solostar)'; non-preferred: 'Basaglar, Basaglar Tempo, Rezvoglar, Semglee-YFGN vial/pen, Toujeo Solostar, Toujeo Max Solostar, Tresiba'. Mixes preferred: 'Humalog Mix 50/50 pen, insulin lispro 75/25 mix pen (gen Humalog Mix), insulin aspart prot/insulin aspart vial/pen (gen Novolog Mix)'; non-preferred: 'Humalog Mix 75/25 pen/vial, Novolog Mix vial/pen'. Rapid-Acting preferred: 'insulin aspart cartridge/vial/pen (gen Novolog), insulin lispro vial/pen (gen Humalog U100), insulin lispro junior (gen Humalog Jr.)'; non-preferred: 'Admelog, Afrezza, Apidra, Fiasp Penfill/FlexTouch/Pumpcart/vial, Humalog Jr. 100 U/mL Kwikpen, Humalog 100 U/mL vial/pen/cartridge/Tempo, Humalog 200 U/mL, Kirsty, Lyumjev, Lyumjev Tempo, Merilog Solostar/vial, Novolog cartridge/vial/FlexPen'. This class is genuinely brand-specific per the task's own framing note; NYRx confirms preference for the authorized-generic/interchangeable-biosimilar glargine and generic aspart/lispro products over their reference brands.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the Excellus BCBS 2026 Formulary Guide (2981) PDF, effective/current as of 7/1/2026 (page 5). The 'INSULIN RESPONSE ENHANCERS - BIGUANIDES' subsection on document page 73 lists: metformin oral tablet 1,000 mg, 500 mg, 850 mg Tier 1 PV, and metformin oral tablet extended release (explicitly labeled in the document as 'generic version of glucophage xr') Tier 1 PV. No brand-name metformin product (e.g., Glucophage, Fortamet, Glumetza) appears anywhere in the document, and no PA or step-therapy flag applies. 500 mg is used as the representative strength since it is the standard starting dose per ADA/guideline dosing. Not verified: nothing further to verify beyond the document text -- this is a straightforward, fully generic class with no restrictions.

records.tier: Tier 2 -- preferred brand (no generic exists for this class)
records.verificationNote: Read directly off the Excellus BCBS 2026 Formulary Guide (2981) PDF, effective/current as of 7/1/2026 (page 5). The 'ANTIHYPERGLYCEMIC - DUAL GIP AND GLP-1 RECEPTOR AGONISTS' and 'ANTIHYPERGLYCEMIC - GLUCAGON-LIKE PEPTIDE-1 (GLP-1) RECEPTOR AGONISTS' subsections on document page 69 list: MOUNJARO Tier 2 PA;QL;PV, OZEMPIC Tier 2 PA;QL;PV, RYBELSUS Tier 2 PA;QL;PV, TRULICITY Tier 2 PA;QL;PV. All four require PA and carry a quantity limit; none has a generic equivalent, so all sit at the same Tier 2 (preferred brand) rather than Tier 1. Ozempic (semaglutide) is selected as the representative preferred agent as the most commonly prescribed T2D GLP-1 in this class; the document itself does not single out one agent over another beyond identical tier/restriction flags -- this is a judgment call, not a distinction the document makes explicitly. Liraglutide (e.g., Victoza) does not appear anywhere in this document, suggesting it is non-formulary or has been fully displaced by the newer agents listed. Not verified: the specific PA clinical criteria (e.g., A1c threshold, prior metformin trial, diagnosis of T2D vs. weight-loss-only use) are not disclosed in this member-facing guide.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the Excellus BCBS 2026 Formulary Guide (2981) PDF, effective/current as of 7/1/2026 (page 5). The 'ANTIHYPERGLYCEMIC - SODIUM GLUCOSE COTRANSPORTER-2 (SGLT2) INHIBITORS' subsection on document page 70 lists exactly two items: dapagliflozin 10 mg tablet Tier 1 QL, FARXIGA Tier 2 QL;PV. A full-text search of the entire 143-page PDF for 'JARDIANCE', 'empagliflozin', 'INVOKANA', and 'canagliflozin' returned zero matches anywhere in the document -- confirming these agents are absent from the formulary entirely, not merely placed at a higher tier. Generic dapagliflozin is used as the representative preferred agent. No PA or step-therapy flag on either dapagliflozin product. Not verified: whether empagliflozin/canagliflozin absence reflects a deliberate P&T Committee exclusion (non-formulary with no coverage pathway) versus simply being outside this document's scope of 'commonly prescribed' medications (the guide states on page 3 that it lists only the most commonly prescribed drugs, not an exhaustive list) -- flagged here as nonformulary based on complete absence from the document, but a formulary exception process may still apply per the plan's standard exception language on page 4.

Coverage is steered entirely to the Lilly (Humalog, Humulin) and Sanofi (Lantus, Toujeo, Basaglar) product families plus an unbranded insulin glargine biosimilar.

records.verificationNote: Read directly off the Excellus BCBS 2026 Formulary Guide (2981) PDF, effective/current as of 7/1/2026 (page 5). The 'HUMAN INSULINS' and 'INSULIN ANALOGS' subsections span document page 73: HUMULIN 70/30 U-100 (vial and KwikPen) Tier 2 PV, HUMULIN N NPH (KwikPen and vial) Tier 2 PV, HUMULIN R Regular U-100 Tier 2 PV, HUMULIN R U-500 (CONC) KwikPen Tier 2 PV, HUMALOG MIX 50-50/75-25 KwikPen and vial Tier 2 PV, insulin lispro protamin-lispro Tier 2 PV, BASAGLAR KwikPen U-100 Tier 3 PV, insulin glargine U-300 conc Tier 2 PV, insulin glargine-yfgn Tier 2 PV, LANTUS SoloStar/U-100 Tier 2 PV, TOUJEO Max/SoloStar U-300 Tier 2 PV, APIDRA 100 unit/mL vial Tier 3 ST;PV, HUMALOG Junior/KwikPen/Tempo Pen/U-100 Tier 2 PV, insulin lispro Tier 2 PV. A full-text search of the entire 143-page PDF for 'NOVOLOG', 'TRESIBA', 'aspart', and 'degludec' returned zero direct-product matches -- NovoLog/Fiasp are mentioned only as compatible pen options for the InPen smart insulin pen device (pages 93, 110), not as separately covered insulin products, and Xultophy (degludec-liraglutide) contains degludec within a combination product even though standalone Tresiba/degludec does not appear. This is treated as a real formulary gap (Novo Nordisk insulin non-preferred/absent) rather than an extraction error, confirmed by the repeated, consistent absence across a full-document search. Insulin glargine (Lantus) + insulin lispro (Humalog) are used as the representative basal+rapid preferred pair, both Tier 2 with only PV flag (no PA). Apidra carries a step-therapy (ST) flag; Basaglar sits at Tier 3 despite being a glargine biosimilar, likely reflecting a rebate/contracting decision rather than a clinical one. Not verified: the exact ST criteria for Apidra (which agents must be tried first, for how long) are not disclosed in this member-facing guide; whether the plan's exception/PA process would still grant Novolog/Tresiba coverage on a case-by-case basis despite their absence from this document.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the UnitedHealthcare 2026 Commercial PDL, effective January 1, 2026, pages 30-31, section 'Diabetes - Non-Insulin Agents.' Line items: 'metformin hcl oral tablet 1000 mg, 500 mg, 850 mg -- 1' (Tier 1, no restriction); 'metformin hcl oral tablet 625 mg, 750 mg -- E' (excluded/PA for these specific strengths); 'metformin hcl er -- 1' (Tier 1); 'metformin hcl er (mod) -- E PA'; 'metformin hcl er (osm) -- E PA'; 'GLUMETZA ORAL TABLET EXTENDED RELEASE 24 HOUR 1000 MG, 500 MG -- E PA'. Per the page-5 legend, 'E' means 'may be excluded from coverage or subject to Prior Authorization in Connecticut, New Jersey and New York' and 'PA' is the standalone prior-authorization symbol -- both appear together on the ER (mod)/(osm) and GLUMETZA lines. Standard immediate-release metformin at common strengths is clean Tier 1 with no restriction.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the UnitedHealthcare 2026 Commercial PDL, effective January 1, 2026, pages 30-31, section 'Diabetes - Non-Insulin Agents.' Line items: 'OZEMPIC -- 2 PA, QL'; 'TRULICITY -- 2 PA, QL'; 'MOUNJARO -- 2 PA, QL'; 'RYBELSUS -- 2 PA, QL'; 'BYDUREON BCISE AUTOINJECTOR SUBCUTANEOUS AUTO-INJECTOR 2 MG/0.85ML -- 2 PA, QL'; 'liraglutide solution pen-injector 18 mg/3ml subcutaneous -- 2 PA, QL (2-pack)' and '-- 3 PA, QL (3-pack)'. Notably, VICTOZA (brand liraglutide) and WEGOVY (semaglutide for weight management) do not appear anywhere in this PDL by name search -- this PDL appears to cover GLP-1 agents only for the type-2-diabetes indication set and may route weight-management-indicated products through a separate obesity-drug policy not captured in this document; that gap is noted rather than assumed. Every GLP-1 in this class carries both PA and QL uniformly -- a clear class-wide restriction, not isolated to non-preferred agents.

Line items: 'JARDIANCE -- 2 QL' (no PA/ST flag); 'FARXIGA -- E ST, QL'; 'DAPAGLIFLOZIN PROPANEDIOL -- E ST, QL'; 'DAPAGLIFLOZIN PRO-METFORMIN ER -- E ST, QL'; 'XIGDUO XR -- E ST, QL'; 'INVOKANA -- E ST, QL'; 'BRENZAVVY -- 3 ST, QL'; 'TRIJARDY XR -- 2 QL'; 'SYNJARDY -- 2 QL'; 'SYNJARDY XR -- 2 QL'; 'GLYXAMBI -- 2 ST, QL'. This is a clear, verbatim class-wide step-therapy pattern favoring JARDIANCE (and its fixed-dose combinations) over every canagliflozin/dapagliflozin-based product in the class, all of which carry the explicit 'ST' step-therapy symbol per the page-5 legend.

Page 1 confirms 'CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026 ... Last updated 06/01/26' and the plan scope (individual/family, small-group under 51 employees, student health plans). Page 160 (document page 160, labeled '158' in-document), under RESPIRATORY > BETA AGONISTS, lists 'albuterol sulfate inhal aero 108 mcg/act (90mcg base equiv) Tier 1 QL (2 inhalers every 30 days)' as the sole covered HFA rescue inhaler in the class -- no restriction beyond the quantity limit, confirming it as the clean-covered preferred agent. Confirmed by case-insensitive full-document search that ProAir, Ventolin, Proventil, and Xopenex (brand names) do not appear anywhere in the 235-page text extraction, meaning these brands are excluded from the formulary entirely in favor of their generic equivalents (this formulary is generic-first across nearly every class reviewed). Nebulizer solutions, oral albuterol, levalbuterol nebulizer/inhaler forms, and terbutaline are all separately listed Tier 1 with no PA, captured as alternatives. No class-wide step-therapy language appears near this section, and no BOGL (brand-preferred-over-generic) language exists anywhere in the document's legend (page 2) or drug tables.

records.verificationNote: Same source as the SABA record: member.carefirst.com/carefirst-resources/pdf/exchange-formulary-sum7277-2026.pdf, fetched via curl and parsed locally with pypdf (plain WebFetch failed to render readable text). Page 1 confirms 'CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026 ... Last updated 06/01/26'. Page 165 (in-document page numbering), under RESPIRATORY > STEROID INHALANTS, lists: ALVESCO 80/160mcg (Tier 3, QL), ASMANEX HFA 50/100/200mcg (Tier 2, QL), budesonide inhalation suspension 0.25/0.5/1mg/2ml (Tier 1, QL, nebulized), and fluticasone furoate aerosol powder breath-activated 50/100/200mcg (Tier 1, QL) -- all read verbatim from the Drug Name/Drug Tier/Requirements columns. Fluticasone furoate was selected as the preferred agent because it is the only Tier 1 (lowest-cost) dry-powder-inhaler-form ICS monotherapy option suitable for standard adult/adolescent maintenance dosing (budesonide nebulizer suspension is typically reserved for young children who cannot use a handheld inhaler). Confirmed by full-document case-insensitive search that Flovent, QVAR, and Pulmicort brand names do not appear anywhere in the 235-page text, and no beclomethasone product of any kind appears -- consistent with this formulary's pattern of excluding discontinued/superseded brands in favor of generic and covered-brand alternatives. No class-wide step-therapy or BOGL language found near this section.

records.verificationNote: Same source: member.carefirst.com/carefirst-resources/pdf/exchange-formulary-sum7277-2026.pdf, fetched via curl and parsed locally with pypdf. Page 1 confirms 'CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026 ... Last updated 06/01/26'. Pages 165-166 (in-document numbering), under RESPIRATORY > STEROID/BETA-AGONIST COMBINATIONS, list verbatim: AIRSUPRA 90-80mcg (Tier 2, QL), BREO ELLIPTA 50-25/100-25/200-25mcg (Tier 2, QL), 'breyna' (Tier 1, QL -- an FDA-approved authorized generic name for budesonide-formoterol, listed lowercase per the document's own generic-vs-brand typographic convention explained on page 2), budesonide-formoterol fumarate dihydrate 80-4.5 and 160-4.5 mcg/act (Tier 1, QL), and fluticasone-salmeterol aerosol powder breath-activated 100-50/250-50/500-50 mcg/act (Tier 1, QL). Selected budesonide-formoterol 160-4.5 (higher strength, twice-daily dosing) as the preferred agent since it is a Tier 1 generic covering the most common combination-inhaler use case (moderate-severe asthma/COPD maintenance). Confirmed by full-document case-insensitive search that Symbicort, Advair, Wixela, AirDuo, and Dulera brand names do not appear anywhere in the 235-page text -- Dulera's absence is notable since no mometasone-formoterol combination product (branded or generic) exists in this formulary at all, making it a hard non-formulary exclusion rather than a generic substitution. No class-wide step-therapy or BOGL language found near this section.

records.verificationNote: Same source: member.carefirst.com/carefirst-resources/pdf/exchange-formulary-sum7277-2026.pdf, fetched via curl and parsed locally with pypdf. Page 1 confirms 'CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026 ... Last updated 06/01/26'. Page 159-160 (in-document numbering), under RESPIRATORY > ANTICHOLINERGICS, lists verbatim: ipratropium bromide inhalation solution 0.02% (Tier 1, QL), SPIRIVA RESPIMAT 1.25mcg/2.5mcg (Tier 2, QL), and tiotropium bromide inhalation capsule 18mcg base equivalent (Tier 1, QL) -- no PA or step-therapy code appears on any of these lines. The combination LAMA products (ANTICHOLINERGIC/BETA AGONIST COMBINATIONS on page 158: BEVESPI, ipratropium-albuterol nebulizer, STIOLTO; and ANTICHOLINERGIC/BETA AGONIST/STEROID COMBINATIONS: BREZTRI, TRELEGY) are all Tier 2 brand with QL only, no PA. Selected tiotropium bromide 18mcg capsule as the preferred agent since it is the sole Tier 1 (generic, lowest-cost) LAMA monotherapy option and is FDA-indicated for COPD maintenance (not asthma monotherapy, consistent with the class definition). Confirmed by full-document case-insensitive search that Anoro Ellipta, Incruse Ellipta (umeclidinium), and Tudorza (aclidinium) do not appear anywhere in the document and are tagged nonformulary above -- no generic exists for umeclidinium or aclidinium in the US market as of this review, so their complete absence means CareFirst does not cover those specific molecules in any form (this is a real coverage gap, not simply a brand-vs-generic substitution pattern seen elsewhere in the document). No class-wide step-therapy or BOGL language found near this section.

Serevent Diskus (LABA monotherapy, not an ICS/LABA combo but included as a covered-with-restriction alternative in the same SYMPATHOMIMETICS subsection) requires step therapy. Chose budesonide-formoterol 160-4.5 as representative preferred agent as the highest-strength, most commonly prescribed maintenance dose; sig is standard clinical convention, not printed verbatim in the formulary (which lists only drug name + UM flags).

Read verbatim from the 'Angiotensin-converting Enzyme (ACE) Inhibitors' subsection under 'Cardiovascular Agents' (PDF page 61, extracted lines 7539-7572): 'Benazepril HCl' G Tier 1, no code; 'Captopril' G Tier 2, QL; 'Enalapril Maleate' G Tier 2, QL; 'Fosinopril Sodium' G Tier 1, no code; 'Lisinopril' G Tier 1, QL; 'Moexipril HCl' G Tier 2, no code; 'Perindopril Erbumine' G Tier 2, no code; 'Quinapril HCl' G Tier 1, no code; 'Ramipril' G Tier 1, no code; 'Trandolapril' G Tier 2, no code. All nine ACE inhibitor generics are covered with no PA or step-therapy anywhere in the class -- confirmed by full-text search that no brand name (Zestril, Prinivil, Vasotec, Lotensin, Monopril, Univasc, Aceon, Accupril, Altace, Mavik, Capoten) appears anywhere in the 130-page document, meaning this class is fully generic-substituted. Selected lisinopril as preferred agent per national first-line guideline convention and its Tier 1 placement; its QL restriction (confirmed in the QL appendix, page 106 of the extracted text: 'Maximum of 2 tablets per day') is captured as the preferredRestriction. No class-wide step-therapy criterion found near this section.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Maryland Medicaid Pharmacy Program Preferred Drug List, Effective Date 7/1/2026, page 20 of 33, section 'ENDOCRINE' > 'Hypoglycemics, Metformins'. Preferred column (verbatim) lists glipizide/metformin (Metaglip), glyburide/metformin (Glucovance), metformin (Glucophage), and metformin ER (Glucophage XR) -- all with no PA/QL flag. Non-Preferred / Requires Prior Authorization column (verbatim) lists metformin 625 mg and 750 mg tablets, metformin ER (Fortamet) with cc,ql, metformin ER (Glumetza) with cc,ql, and metformin solution (Riomet). Standard 500/850/1000mg IR metformin and standard-strength ER (Glucophage XR) are the clean preferred formulations; only the odd-dose 625/750mg tablets and two specific branded ER products (Fortamet, Glumetza) plus the oral solution are restricted. Metformin IR 500mg chosen as the representative preferred strength/form per guideline first-line convention (the PDL itself does not rank within its preferred list). Tier inferred (partial) as standard preferred generic; no explicit tier/copay level stated in the PDL.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Maryland Medicaid Pharmacy Program Preferred Drug List, Effective Date 7/1/2026, pages 19-20 of 33, section 'ENDOCRINE' > 'Hypoglycemics, Incretin Mimetics and Enhancers'. This PDL row bundles GLP-1 receptor agonists together with DPP-4 inhibitors (a related but distinct oral incretin-based drug class); only the GLP-1/dual-agonist agents relevant to this class definition are extracted here (DPP-4 inhibitors Onglyza, Glyxambi, Januvia, Janumet, Jentadueto, Tradjenta, alogliptin, sitagliptin are out of scope for this glp1 class file). Preferred column (verbatim) explicitly lists liraglutide (Victoza, ql), Mounjaro, Ozempic, and Trulicity with no PA/QL flag beyond Victoza's quantity limit. Non-Preferred / Requires Prior Authorization column (verbatim, GLP-1-relevant items) lists exenatide (Byetta), Rybelsus, Soliqua, and Xultophy. Wegovy (semaglutide for weight management) does not appear anywhere on this PDL page and is marked nonformulary/unconfirmed rather than assumed covered, since Medicaid weight-loss-indication GLP-1 coverage is frequently carved out or handled under separate state policy not visible in this document. Ozempic (semaglutide) selected as the representative preferred agent given its prominence in the class definition (semaglutide, dulaglutide, liraglutide, tirzepatide) and being explicitly brand-listed as preferred; the PDL does not rank within its own preferred list. Tier inferred (partial) as standard preferred brand; no explicit tier/copay level stated in the PDL.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Maryland Medicaid Pharmacy Program Preferred Drug List, Effective Date 7/1/2026, page 20 of 33, section 'ENDOCRINE' > 'Hypoglycemics, Insulins'. Preferred column (verbatim) lists insulin lispro pen/vial (Humalog pen, vial), insulin lispro Junior Kwikpen (Humalog Junior Kwikpen), insulin lispro mix 75/25 pen (Humalog Mix 75/25 pen), Humalog cartridge, Humalog Mix 50/50 pen/vial, Humalog Mix 75/25 vial, Humulin vial, Humulin 70/30 pen/vial, Humulin R U-500 pen, Lantus Solostar, and Lantus Vial -- with no PA/QL flag on any of these. Non-Preferred / Requires Prior Authorization column (verbatim) lists insulin degludec (Tresiba), insulin glargine pen & max pen (Toujeo, Toujeo Max), insulin glargine-YFGN (Semglee-YFGN), Admelog, Afrezza, Apidra, Basaglar/Basaglar Tempo, Fiasp/Fiasp pumpcart, Humalog 200 unit/mL pen, Humalog Tempo, Humulin pen, Kirsty pen/vial, Levemir, Lyumjev/Lyumjev Tempo, Merilog Solostar/vial, Myxredlin, Novolog, Novolog 70/30, Novolin pen/vial, Novolin 70/30, and Rezvoglar Kwikpen. This confirms the class definition's note that formulary preference is brand-specific: Maryland Medicaid's covered basal insulin is specifically Lantus (glargine), not Toujeo/Basaglar/Levemir/Tresiba, and its covered rapid-acting insulin is specifically Humalog (lispro), not Novolog/Fiasp/Apidra/Admelog/Lyumjev. Insulin lispro (Humalog) chosen as the representative preferred rapid-acting agent since the class definition names both a basal and rapid representative; Lantus (glargine) is the preferred basal alternative captured in the alternatives list. Tier inferred (partial); no explicit tier/copay level stated in the PDL beyond the preferred/non-preferred split.

records.verificationNote: Fetched member.carefirst.com/carefirst-resources/pdf/exchange-formulary-sum7277-2026.pdf via curl and parsed locally with pypdf. Page 1 confirms 'CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026 ... Last updated 06/01/26'. Page 103 (in-document numbering), under ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC > ANTIDIABETICS, BIGUANIDE, lists verbatim: metformin hcl tab 500mg (Tier 1, blank Requirements), metformin hcl tab 850mg (Tier 1, '$0 copay for members age 35-70 for prevention of diabetes'), metformin hcl tab 1000mg (Tier 1, blank), and metformin hcl tab er 24hr 500mg/750mg (Tier 1, blank) -- no PA, ST, or QL applies to plain metformin at any strength or release formulation. Selected metformin hcl 500mg IR as the preferred agent as the guideline-standard starting dose/formulation. The metformin-containing combination products (glipizide-metformin, pioglitazone-metformin) are also Tier 1 with no restriction; the alogliptin-metformin combination carries 'ST; PA**' (step therapy, with PA applying if step is not met) per its own line -- this restriction applies to the DPP-4 component of that combination product, not to metformin itself. Confirmed by full-document case-insensitive search that no brand name (Glucophage, Glucophage XR, Fortamet, Glumetza, Riomet) appears anywhere in the document -- fully generic-substituted class with zero brand presence and no molecule/dose absent from the formulary, so paRequired is empty. No class-wide step-therapy applies to metformin monotherapy itself; no BOGL language found near this section.

records.verificationNote: Fetched member.carefirst.com/carefirst-resources/pdf/exchange-formulary-sum7277-2026.pdf via curl and parsed locally with pypdf. Page 1 confirms 'CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026 ... Last updated 06/01/26'. Page 104 (in-document numbering), under ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC > ANTIDIABETICS, INCRETIN MIMETIC AGENTS, lists verbatim: liraglutide soln pen-injector 18mg/3ml (Tier 1, 'PA, QL (3 pens every 30 days)'), MOUNJARO INJ all six strengths (Tier 2, 'PA, QL (4 pens every 28 days)'), OZEMPIC INJ all three strengths (Tier 2, 'PA, QL (3 mL every 28 days)'), and TRULICITY INJ all four strengths (Tier 2, 'PA, QL (4 pens every 28 days)') -- PA applies even to the Tier 1 generic liraglutide, so this class carries a PA requirement on the preferred agent itself, captured in preferredRestriction. The ANTIDIABETICS, INCRETIN MIMETIC COMBINATION AGENTS subsection immediately below lists SOLIQUA and XULTOPHY (both Tier 2, blank Requirements column -- no PA noted for the combination products themselves). Selected generic liraglutide as the preferred agent since it is the only Tier 1 option in the class, despite carrying its own PA requirement -- this is captured explicitly in preferredRestriction per the task instructions. Confirmed by full-document case-insensitive search that Rybelsus, Wegovy, Zepbound, Victoza, Saxenda, Byetta, and Bydureon do not appear anywhere in the 235-page text; separately confirmed no 'weight loss', 'anti-obesity', or 'obesity agents' section header exists anywhere in the document, meaning CareFirst's Exchange formulary does not cover any anti-obesity-indicated GLP-1/GIP product at all (a hard exclusion, not a brand-vs-generic substitution). No class-wide step-therapy language found; no BOGL note applies since there is no generic-of-a-brand relationship being reversed here (liraglutide is itself the generic).

records.verificationNote: Fetched member.carefirst.com/carefirst-resources/pdf/exchange-formulary-sum7277-2026.pdf via curl and parsed locally with pypdf. Page 1 confirms 'CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026 ... Last updated 06/01/26'. Page 106 (in-document numbering), under ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC > ANTIDIABETICS, SODIUM-GLUCOSE COTRANSPORTER-2 (SGLT2) INHIBITORS, lists verbatim only: 'JARDIANCE TAB 10MG Tier 2 ST; PA**; Indicated for Diabetes and Heart Failure' and 'JARDIANCE TAB 25MG Tier 2 ST; PA**; Indicated for Diabetes and Heart Failure' -- Jardiance is the ONLY SGLT2 monotherapy molecule present in the entire document. The adjacent SGLT2 INHIBITOR COMBINATIONS subsection lists only SYNJARDY/SYNJARDY XR (empagliflozin/metformin, Tier 2, ST;PA**) and the SGLT2/DPP-4 COMBINATIONS subsection lists only GLYXAMBI (empagliflozin/linagliptin, Tier 2, ST;PA**) -- every SGLT2-containing product on this formulary is empagliflozin-based; no dapagliflozin- or canagliflozin- or ertugliflozin-based product of any kind (monotherapy or combination) appears anywhere. Jardiance was selected as the (mandatory, single-option) preferred agent -- there is no Tier 1 generic SGLT2 inhibitor since none is FDA-approved/marketed as generic yet. The restriction is captured on the preferred agent itself (preferredRestriction) since even Jardiance carries ST;PA** per its own line, not just the excluded competitors. Confirmed by full-document case-insensitive search that Farxiga, Invokana, Steglatro, Xigduo, and Qtern do not appear anywhere in the 235-page text -- this is a single-molecule-covered class, an unusually narrow formulary design worth flagging plainly rather than treating as a typical brand/generic pattern.

records.verificationNote: Fetched member.carefirst.com/carefirst-resources/pdf/exchange-formulary-sum7277-2026.pdf via curl and parsed locally with pypdf. Page 1 confirms 'CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026 ... Last updated 06/01/26'. Pages 104-105 (in-document numbering), under ENDOCRINE AND METABOLIC > ANTIDIABETICS, INSULIN, list verbatim: BASAGLAR KWP/TMP INJ 100/ML (Tier 2, blank), FIASP FLEX/INJ/PENFIL/PMPCRT (Tier 2, blank), GLARGIN YFGN INJ/SOL 100U/ML (Tier 2, blank), HUMULIN INJ 70/30, 70/30KWP, N U-100, N U-100KWP, R U-100 (Tier 3, 'OTC'), HUMULIN R INJ U-500/U-500KWP (Tier 2, blank), NOVOLIN INJ 70/30, 70/30 FP, N 100/U-100, R 100/U-100 (Tier 2, 'OTC; RELION not covered'), NOVOLOG INJ/FLEXPEN/PENFILL/MIX (Tier 2, blank), and TRESIBA FLEX INJ 100/200UNIT, INJ 100UNIT (Tier 2, blank) -- no PA, ST, or QL restriction appears on any insulin product in this list; 'GLARGIN YFGN' is the FDA nonproprietary designation for the interchangeable biosimilar glargine product (marketed as Semglee/Rezvoglar) and is selected as the preferred agent for basal glargine since it is the newest/lowest-list-price interchangeable option, though Basaglar carries identical no-PA coverage. Selected insulin glargine-yfgn as the single preferred agent representing the basal category (note: this class genuinely spans both basal and rapid-acting needs -- Novolog/Fiasp are the covered rapid-acting options, captured as alternatives, since the requested schema asks for one preferred agent per class). Confirmed by full-document case-insensitive search that Lantus, Semglee (brand name), Toujeo, Humalog, Admelog, Lyumjev, and Apidra do not appear anywhere in the 235-page text -- a substantial and clinically significant nonformulary list, since Humalog and Lantus are among the most commonly prescribed insulins nationally; CareFirst's Exchange formulary has narrowed to Basaglar/glargine-yfgn for basal and Novolog/Fiasp for rapid-acting. No class-wide step-therapy or BOGL language found near this section; the 'RELION not covered' note is a real formulary-specific restriction on Walmart's private-label human insulin, captured verbatim in the Novolin alternatives note.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Priority Partners (690) MCO Formulary, effective 07/01/2026, pages 35-36, section 'ANTIDIABETICS > INSULIN'. Confirmed via PyMuPDF text extraction of the downloaded PDF (page-1 caption 'Priority Partners (690) / Effective 07/01/2026'). The formulary lists insulin by brand/product code rather than by INN, consistent with insulin's brand-specific coverage norm noted in the class description. Basal insulins (LANTUS/glargine, GLARGIN YFGN biosimilar) and rapid-acting insulins (HUMALOG/lispro, NOVOLOG/aspart, ADMELOG/lispro, FIASP/faster aspart) are all covered with quantity limits only, no PA, for the standard vial and pen delivery forms. A distinct age-based restriction pattern appears specifically on the older human-insulin KwikPen delivery devices (Humulin and Novolin brand pens): covered without restriction for patients under 18, but requiring PA for adults — this is a delivery-device-specific PA, not a molecule-level restriction, and does not apply to the vial forms of the same insulins. Chose the interchangeable glargine biosimilar (marketed as unbranded 'Semglee'/listed as GLARGIN YFGN in the formulary) as the representative preferred basal agent since FDA-designated interchangeable biosimilars are the lowest-cost basal option and are listed ahead of brand Lantus in the document; verification of directions-for-use text is inferred from standard insulin glargine prescribing practice since the formulary itself lists only product name and UM flags, not sigs.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Maryland Medicaid Pharmacy Program Preferred Drug List, Effective Date 7/1/2026, page 4 of 33, section 'ANALGESICS' > 'Nonsteroidal Anti-Inflammatories (NSAIDS)'. Preferred column (verbatim) lists celecoxib (Celebrex), diclofenac sodium, diclofenac gel (Voltaren Gel), ibuprofen Rx/OTC (Motrin), indomethacin (Indocin), meloxicam tablets (Mobic), nabumetone (Relafen), naproxen, naproxen sodium OTC, and sulindac (Clinoril) -- all with no PA/QL flag; this is an unusually broad preferred list with no single ranked 'top' agent identified by the source. Non-Preferred / Requires Prior Authorization column (verbatim) lists a long roster of alternate NSAIDs and formulations: diclofenac epolamine patch (Flector), diclofenac potassium capsule/tablet, diclofenac/misoprostol (Arthrotec), diclofenac SR (Voltaren XL), diflunisal (Dolobid), etodolac/etodolac XL (Lodine, Lodine XL), fenoprofen, flurbiprofen, ibuprofen 300mg (cc,ql), ibuprofen chewable OTC, ibuprofen/famotidine (Duexis), indomethacin ER (Indocin SR), indomethacin rectal, ketoprofen/ketoprofen ER (Orudis, Oruvail), ketorolac (Toradol), meclofenamate (Meclomen), mefenamic acid (Ponstel), meloxicam capsules (Vivlodex), naproxen/esomeprazole (Vimovo), oxaprozin (Daypro), piroxicam (Feldene), tolmetin sodium, and branded products Coxanto Patch, Licart, Lofena, Relafen DS, Vyscoxa. Naproxen chosen as the representative preferred agent per standard first-line-oral-NSAID convention (matches the class definition's own example ordering); the PDL itself does not distinguish a single top pick among its nine clean-preferred generics. Tier inferred (partial) as standard preferred generic; no explicit tier/copay level stated in the PDL.

records.verificationNote: Fetched member.carefirst.com/carefirst-resources/pdf/exchange-formulary-sum7277-2026.pdf via curl and parsed locally with pypdf (plain WebFetch returned only unparsed binary PDF stream data). Page 1 confirms 'CareFirst Exchange Formulary 2026 ... Last updated 06/01/26'. Pages 1-2 (in-document numbering 1-2), under ANALGESICS > COX-2 INHIBITORS and NSAIDS, list verbatim: celecoxib 50/100/200mg (Tier 1, blank), diclofenac potassium 50mg (Tier 1, blank), diclofenac sodium gel 1% (Tier 1, 'QL (300g every 30 days), OTC'), diclofenac sodium delayed-release/ER tablets (Tier 1, blank), etodolac all forms (Tier 1, blank), flurbiprofen (Tier 1, blank), ibuprofen suspension/tablets (Tier 1, blank), ketorolac tromethamine tab 10mg (Tier 1, 'QL (20 tabs every 30 days)'), meclofenamate sodium, mefenamic acid, meloxicam 7.5/15mg (Tier 1, blank), nabumetone (Tier 1, blank), naproxen 250/375/500mg (Tier 1, blank), oxaprozin, piroxicam, sulindac (all Tier 1, blank), voltaren arthritis pain gel (Tier 1, 'QL (300g every 30 days), OTC'), and the NSAIDS, COMBINATIONS subsection listing diclofenac w/ misoprostol delayed-release 50-0.2mg and 75-0.2mg (Tier 1, blank). Every single oral NSAID in the formulary is Tier 1 with no PA or step-therapy restriction; only ketorolac (a short-course-only NSAID due to GI/renal risk) and the topical diclofenac gel carry quantity limits, which are standard clinical safety guardrails rather than access barriers. Selected naproxen 500mg as the preferred agent per common first-line convention for musculoskeletal pain; the entire class being Tier 1 no-PA means there is no meaningfully differentiated 'preferred' vs 'alternative' status -- all are equally accessible, captured as alternatives. Confirmed by full-document case-insensitive search that no brand NSAID (Celebrex is absent as a brand name -- generic celecoxib is listed only; Mobic, Anaprox, Naprosyn, Voltaren Gel is present as a specific branded OTC product line item alongside generic diclofenac gel) creates any PA/ST barrier; there is no drug in the requested oral-NSAID list that is excluded, so paRequired is empty. No class-wide step-therapy or BOGL language found near this section.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Priority Partners (690) MCO Formulary, effective 07/01/2026, pages 21-22, section 'NONSTEROIDAL ANTI-INFLAMMATORY AGENTS (NSAIDS)' (nested under the ANALGESICS - ANTI-INFLAMMATORY parent heading). Confirmed via PyMuPDF text extraction of the downloaded PDF (page-1 caption 'Priority Partners (690) / Effective 07/01/2026'). Every single oral NSAID entry in this section — naproxen (all salts/forms), ibuprofen (all forms), meloxicam, celecoxib, diclofenac (both salts, all release forms), etodolac, flurbiprofen, indomethacin, ketorolac, mefenamic acid, nabumetone, oxaprozin, and sulindac — is listed with zero PA/ST/QL/nonformulary flag in the extracted text; this is a fully open, all-generic-covered class with no gating whatsoever. Naproxen 500mg chosen as representative preferred agent as the most commonly prescribed first-line oral NSAID for musculoskeletal pain per clinical convention (this text-based sig is inferred from standard prescribing information, not printed verbatim in the formulary, which lists only drug name).

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the UnitedHealthcare 2026 PDL, effective January 1, 2026, page 8 (Analgesics - Drugs for Pain and Inflammation). Confirmed: 'meloxicam oral tablet 1', 'naproxen oral tablet 1', 'naproxen oral tablet delayed release 1', 'ibuprofen oral tablet 400 mg, 600 mg, 800 mg 1', 'diclofenac sodium oral 1', 'nabumetone oral 1', 'sulindac oral 1', 'indomethacin oral capsule 1', 'ketorolac tromethamine oral 1', 'ec-naproxen 1' -- all Tier 1. Confirmed Tier 2: 'celecoxib oral 2', 'naproxen sodium oral tablet 275 mg, 550 mg 2', 'etodolac 2', 'oxaprozin oral tablet 2', 'piroxicam oral 2', 'indomethacin er 2', 'diclofenac potassium oral tablet 50 mg 2'. Confirmed PA/exclusion-flagged ('E'): 'CELEBREX E', 'NAPROSYN E', 'ANAPROX DS E', 'LODINE E', 'RELAFEN DS E', 'diclofenac potassium oral tablet 25 mg E QL', 'LOFENA E QL', 'ibuprofen oral suspension 100 mg/5ml E'. Confirmed Tier 3/4 non-preferred: 'diclofenac sodium er 3', 'diclofenac-misoprostol 3', 'EC-NAPROSYN ORAL TABLET DELAYED RELEASE 375 MG 3', 'EC-NAPROSYN ORAL TABLET DELAYED RELEASE 500 MG 4', 'DAYPRO 4', 'FELDENE ORAL CAPSULE 20 MG 4'. This class is entirely generic-dominated with no PA/step therapy on any Tier 1/2 generic -- consistent with oral NSAIDs being OTC-adjacent, low-cost first-line agents. Sig reflects standard meloxicam FDA-label dosing, not stated verbatim in the PDL.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the Cigna Healthcare Value 4-Tier Prescription Drug List, coverage starting 07/01/2026 (list last updated 04/01/2026), page 22 of 33, section 'PAIN RELIEF AND INFLAMMATORY DISEASE'. Confirmed verbatim: 'celecoxib 1 QL', 'diclofenac 1% gel, tablet 1 QL', 'indomethacin capsule, oral suspension, 50 mg suppository 1', 'meloxicam tablet 1', 'nabumetone 1', and 'ketorolac syringe, tablet, vial 1 QL' all appear at Tier 1 with no PA/ST beyond the noted QL on some agents. A full-document text search for 'naproxen' and 'ibuprofen' (both generic and any brand variant) returned zero matches across all 33 pages -- these two of the most commonly prescribed oral NSAIDs are entirely absent from this drug list, which is consistent with the document's own stated policy (page 3) of excluding certain OTC-available drug classes from prescription coverage, though that page-3 note explicitly names only allergy and heartburn OTC classes, not NSAIDs by name -- flagging this inference as the least-certain part of this record. Meloxicam selected as representative preferred agent (once-daily dosing, common first-line oral NSAID choice) among the multiple clean Tier-1 options; the source itself does not rank within the Tier-1 generics. Representative strength and standard once-daily sig for meloxicam 15 mg are standard clinical convention, not stated verbatim in the PDL (drug/tier/notes format only); tier level is read directly from the document.

records.verificationNote: Read directly from the '2026 Aetna Pharmacy Drug Guide - Aetna Standard Plan' PDF (doc code 1348063-01-04 (01/26), effective January 2026). ANALGESICS > NSAIDS section (page 3/internal page 1) lists verbatim: 'diclofenac sodium, diclofenac sodium gel 1%, diclofenac sodium solution 1.5%, ibuprofen, meloxicam tablet, naproxen (except naproxen CR or naproxen suspension)'; the adjacent COX-2 INHIBITORS subsection lists 'celecoxib'; NSAIDS, COMBINATIONS lists 'diclofenac sodium-misoprostol, ibuprofen-famotidine'. The PREFERRED OPTIONS LIST FOR FORMULARY DRUG REMOVALS section confirms CELEBREX is removed, redirected verbatim to 'celecoxib, diclofenac sodium, ibuprofen, meloxicam tablet, naproxen (except naproxen CR or naproxen suspension)', and separately confirms multiple diclofenac-salt-form removal entries (diclofenac potassium capsule/powder) redirected to the sodium-salt generic tablet forms, plus ARTHROTEC redirected to an NSAID-plus-PPI combination. Naproxen (generic, IR tablet) was selected as representative preferred agent among the several equally-listed oral NSAID generics since it is a first-line guideline choice with favorable cardiovascular safety profile among NSAIDs; the Drug Guide does not rank the generics against each other. Sig/strength are representative standard naproxen dosing, not printed verbatim in the Drug Guide (which lists drug names only, no dosing) - marked inferred.
